challenges.prompt: A user asks the agent for churn-risk customers. The agent sends this over the wire to your server. Which primitive is being used, and who decided to send it?
options: A Resource read — the server decided to push it on a schedule.
A Tool call — the agent (host) decided when to call it, based on the user's request.
A Prompt template — the database administrator triggered it.
A raw SQL connection — the model connected to the DB directly.
challenges.explanation: The method is tools/call, so this is a Tool — an action the model can invoke. The agent decides *when* to call it based on the user's request; your server code decides *what* it does. The model never touches the database directly.

challenges.prompt: This run_sql tool is supposed to be a safe boundary that only allows a single read-only SELECT. But it lets a destructive statement through. Which fix restores the boundary?
options: Encrypt the sql string before executing it.
Add: if not clean.lower().startswith("select") or ";" in clean: return "Error: only a single read-only SELECT is allowed."
Give the model the database credentials so it can validate the query itself.
Wrap the call in a try/except and ignore any errors.
challenges.explanation: The server is the boundary — it must validate input before touching the data. Checking that the statement starts with SELECT and contains no extra semicolon rejects writes and chained statements. Encryption and try/except don't stop a DROP or DELETE, and handing the model credentials removes the boundary entirely.

challenges.prompt: Complete this FastMCP server so it exposes an action the model can invoke and a piece of read-only context.
challenges.explanation: @mcp.tool() exposes a Tool — an action the model can invoke. @mcp.resource(...) exposes a Resource — read-only context the agent can pull in. Together with Prompts, these are the three primitives that make up a server's entire surface area.

challenges.prompt: Put the four MCP architecture layers in order, from the app the user talks to, all the way down to the real data.
lines: Host — the app the user interacts with; embeds the LLM and one MCP client per server
MCP Client — lives inside the host; speaks JSON-RPC 2.0 and relays tool calls
MCP Server — your connector code; advertises Tools, Resources & Prompts and validates inputs
Data / Service — the actual DB, API, or filesystem the server gatekeeps
challenges.explanation: Host → MCP Client → MCP Server → Data/Service. The host embeds the model and a client; the client speaks the protocol; your server is the validating boundary; the data lives behind it. This chain is exactly why the model never holds credentials — every request passes through your server first.
